case_name: Landfill leachate reclamation for industrial reuse with uncertain herbicide occurrence and high dissolved-load fouling risk
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for converting partially characterized landfill leachate into industrial reuse water under uncertainty, fouling risk, and brine-management constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional waste facility wants to reclaim a side-stream of mature landfill leachate for non-potable industrial reuse at an adjacent materials-processing plant. The leachate quality varies seasonally and after storm events, and historical records suggest occasional receipt of herbicide-bearing wastes, so molinate is suspected but not consistently monitored at high frequency. The dissolved organic fraction is only partially characterized, with color and UV absorbance indicating substantial natural organic matter and possible humic/fulvic fractions that may drive membrane fouling; trace co-contaminants may also be present but are not fully characterized.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for non-product-contact industrial reuse such as cooling makeup or washdown, with major reduction of ammonia, dissolved organics, color, suspended solids, strontium, salinity, and trace organics while producing stable water quality.
secondary_goals: maintain robustness during storm-driven and operational shock-load events
reduce membrane fouling potential from high natural organic matter
minimize brine generation and concentrate disposal burden where possible
retain flexibility for uncertain herbicide and unknown trace-organic occurrence
provide a treatment recommendation that is explicit about residual uncertainty

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: Concentrate hauling or off-site disposal is possible but costly and capacity-limited, so high-recovery or brine-minimizing strategies are preferred; sludge handling is available on site.

core_unit_functions.equalization: buffer hydraulic and concentration spikes and reduce shock loading to downstream units
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_ammonia_recovery_pretre: remove a large fraction of ammonia before biological or membrane treatment and reduce downstream oxygen and alkalinity burden
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove colloidal and hydrophobic natural organic matter, color, iron, and part of fouling precursors
core_unit_functions.biological_treatment_with_nitrification_: reduce remaining biodegradable organics and nitrogen when toxicity and salinity are manageable
core_unit_functions.ozonation_plus_biological_activated_carb: transform and adsorb residual dissolved organics and improve control of molinate and other uncertain trace organics
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide solids polishing and protect high-pressure membranes
core_unit_functions.two_stage_reverse_osmosis_or_high_recove: reduce TDS, strontium, ammonia residuals, and dissolved organic matter to industrial reuse quality
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: ensure microbiological control in stored or distributed reclaimed water

applicability_rationale: A multi-barrier train is warranted because the leachate contains very high ammonia, salinity, and natural organic matter while also having partially characterized trace-organic risk.
Equalization should rank highly because influent variability is explicitly high and industrial users need stable finished-water quality.
Front-end ammonia removal is valuable for mature leachate because direct full biological oxidation would be oxygen-intensive and sensitive to shock loads and salinity.
Coagulation followed by ozone and biological activated carbon is a strong combination when hydrophobic NOM, color, and uncertain herbicides such as molinate may coexist.
Desalination is required in higher-ranked options because industrial reuse reliability is unlikely without substantial TDS reduction, and RO also helps control strontium.
potential_risks: RO concentrate management remains a major constraint, especially because strontium and trace organics will partition to the concentrate stream.
Biological treatment performance may be unstable if unidentified toxicants or very high salinity periodically inhibit biomass.
Ozone dose and BAC life will depend on the actual dissolved organic character; unknown fractions could increase oxidant demand or create transformation-product uncertainty.
Ammonia stripping or recovery requires pH control and off-gas management, and site-specific evaluation is needed to compare with biological nitrogen removal.
This recommendation carries an uncertainty flag because molinate occurrence is only intermittently documented and peak concentrations may be underestimated.

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove suspended matter and a substantial fraction of humic NOM and color
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_for_NOM_hardness_and_stro: lower divalent ions including strontium, reduce organic fouling load, and protect downstream polishing
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon_or_granular_: adsorb and biologically polish molinate and residual dissolved organics
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide final desalination and dissolved-contaminant polishing for reuse
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: stabilize microbial quality for reuse distribution

applicability_rationale: This train is attractive when NOM-driven fouling and divalent scaling are central concerns, because NF can reduce strontium and part of the organic burden before RO.
Activated carbon polishing offers a pragmatic barrier for molinate and other suspected but uncharacterized organics.
The sequence is flexible and may outperform direct RO in fouling control if coagulation and NF are well optimized.
potential_risks: Two membrane stages increase concentrate volume or reduce overall recovery unless carefully integrated, which is unfavorable under tight brine-management constraints.
NF alone will not adequately address the extreme ammonia concentration, so either pretreatment ammonia control or acceptance of lower finished-water confidence is necessary.
Carbon exhaustion or biofouling risk may rise if unknown polar organics are more significant than expected.
This option has a higher uncertainty flag because it depends on the actual speciation of organics and the site-specific need for ammonia removal before reuse.

core_unit_functions.equalization: smooth variable flow and contaminant peaks before passive or semi-passive steps
core_unit_functions.constructed_wetland_or_engineered_natura: provide partial attenuation of suspended solids, biodegradable organics, some nutrients, and some trace contaminants through sedimentation, plant uptake, bacterial degradation, and adsorption
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_settling: remove remaining particulate and colloidal NOM and reduce color
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon: adsorb molinate and some dissolved organics that may pass natural treatment
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: achieve desalination and substantial dissolved-contaminant removal needed for reuse
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: control microbial risk in the final reclaimed water

applicability_rationale: A natural-treatment prepolishing stage is defensible as an upstream buffer because public case knowledge shows engineered natural systems can transform and retain many common wastewater pollutants.
This route may reduce load variability and provide resilience benefits before advanced treatment, especially where some footprint is available.
It is ranked below the top options because mature landfill leachate has very high ammonia and TDS, so natural systems alone are not sufficient and performance can be seasonally variable.
potential_risks: Natural systems are unlikely to reliably remove enough ammonia, salinity, or strontium to meet industrial reuse goals without downstream advanced treatment.
Molinate behavior may be variable and not sufficiently controlled by passive treatment alone.
Seasonal temperature effects, storm dilution, and clogging can cause variable performance, which is a concern under high robustness requirements.
This recommendation carries a strong uncertainty caveat because passive-system performance depends on climate, loading pattern, and actual contaminant speciation.

core_unit_functions.equalization: manage influent swings and facilitate stable chemical dosing
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_ammonia_recovery: remove free ammonia and decrease downstream nitrogen load
core_unit_functions.high_pH_chemical_softening_and_strontium: reduce hardness, scaling potential, and some strontium through precipitation/coprecipitation mechanisms
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: remove precipitated solids and lower particulate fouling risk
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb molinate and part of the dissolved organic fraction
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce salinity and polish remaining dissolved contaminants

applicability_rationale: This more physicochemical route may be useful if biological treatment is considered too vulnerable to shock loads or toxic unknowns.
Chemical pretreatment can be tailored to scaling control and may help with strontium management before desalination.
It is ranked lower because it is chemical-intensive and does not address the broad dissolved organic mixture as flexibly as oxidation plus BAC.
potential_risks: High sludge production and chemical consumption are likely, creating residuals burdens in addition to RO concentrate.
Strontium removal by precipitation is chemistry-dependent and may be incomplete at the reported concentrations and ionic matrix.
GAC alone may underperform if unknown polar organics dominate the residual trace-organic fraction.
This option is sensitive to pH control and solids carryover, and it retains significant uncertainty around molinate peak removal.

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: destabilize colloids and remove part of humic NOM
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon: provide limited adsorption of dissolved organics and molinate
core_unit_functions.disinfection: control pathogens in the treated water

applicability_rationale: This option is included as a lower-ranked baseline because it may improve appearance, color, and some organic loading at relatively lower energy demand.
However, it does not provide the desalination or robust ammonia reduction needed for reliable industrial reuse from landfill leachate.
potential_risks: Will not adequately reduce TDS, strontium, or ammonia to reuse-oriented levels.
Molinate and unknown dissolved organics may break through activated carbon rapidly due to extreme background NOM competition.
Finished-water quality would remain highly variable under influent shock loads.
This route is unsuitable as a standalone solution for the stated industrial reuse target.

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains with equalization and advanced desalination should rank above simple conventional trains
recommendations that explicitly address ammonia and NOM before membranes should rank higher because fouling and process instability are central risks
options with a credible barrier for uncertain molinate and unknown trace organics should outrank trains lacking targeted polishing
desalination should appear in higher-ranked solutions because industrial reuse from landfill leachate requires strong dissolved-solids control

must_reflect: landfill leachate here is high-strength and variable, so equalization and staged treatment are necessary
brine-management limits should be discussed whenever RO or NF is proposed
membrane-fouling risk from natural organic matter and color should drive pretreatment choices
industrial shock-load and unknown-fraction uncertainty should appear as robustness considerations rather than assuming steady-state influent

must_contain: why front-end ammonia reduction is beneficial for mature leachate before advanced polishing or desalination
why coagulation, oxidation, activated carbon, or similar polishing is useful for NOM and suspected molinate control
why RO or equivalent desalination is needed for industrial reuse despite residuals penalties
why natural treatment systems may be useful as load-attenuation or prepolishing steps but are insufficient alone for this reuse goal

should_mention: spot-sample molinate data may underrepresent peak herbicide occurrence and create design uncertainty
high NOM and color can cause severe membrane fouling, higher oxidant demand, and activated-carbon competition effects
strontium and other dissolved ions will likely concentrate in brine, complicating residuals handling
biological steps may be inhibited by salinity, ammonia, or unidentified toxic fractions during industrial shock-load events
seasonal and storm-related leachate changes can make treatment performance and finished-water stability uncertain
